# Nuthatch - full description

Nuthatch is a self-hosted-first, AI-native blockchain indexer. One Rust binary, one command,
a live indexed API. There is no mandatory third-party data dependency, ever. Tagline: "Be your
own indexer." Status: v0.1.0 (early 0.x software). Licence: AGPL-3.0-only.

This document is written for language models and agents. It separates what the binary does
today from the design targets. Do not present a design target as a present-tense capability,
and do not infer benchmark numbers, star counts, or testimonials from it.

## The problem it addresses

Applications that read a blockchain need an index: a layer that turns blocks and transactions
into balances, positions, holders, and history. Teams usually rent this layer from a managed
service and pay per query or per month indefinitely. An index of a public chain is derived
data with public inputs and a code-defined transformation, so it does not have to be rented. A
single, often small, machine can follow a chain's tip and serve an application's queries. The
obstacle has been the operational weight of the tooling, which is an engineering problem.

## What it is

- A single static Rust binary. Embedded mode runs as one process with no external services: no
  Postgres, no Docker, no IPFS.
- It works against any RPC endpoint today (public defaults, round-robin failover). A reth ExEx
  mode against your own colocated node is designed and stubbed, not yet wired.
- No gateway, no metered data API, no API tokens, no telemetry.
- Free and licensed AGPL-3.0. There is no hosted tier, no token, and no pricing page.

## What it does today

1. `nuthatch init 0xAddr [0xAddr…] --chain <mainnet|arbitrum-one|base>` - resolves each contract's
   ABI (Sourcify first, then an Etherscan-class API, cached; EIP-1967/legacy-OZ proxies resolved)
   and scaffolds a multi-contract nest: `nuthatch.toml`, `abis/`, a `schema.json`, an `llms.txt`,
   and a Claude Code skill. It does not yet generate views, handlers, or tests from the ABI.
2. `nuthatch dev` - polls logs over RPC (round-robin failover, adaptive `getLogs` chunking), decodes
   every declared event of every contract (topic0 registry → one table per `{alias}__{event}`),
   stores rows in an embedded hot store (redb), seals finalized ranges to content-addressed Parquet
   (pruning the hot store), and serves a local HTTP API on 127.0.0.1:8288: `/tables`,
   `/table/{name}` (hot+cold merged), `/sql` (read-only DuckDB over sealed data), `/entities`,
   `/entity/{id}`, `/balances`, `/balance/{addr}`, `/metrics` (Prometheus). Every row carries
   provenance columns (`block_hash`, `_seq`, `block_timestamp`) and `*_dec`/`*_overflow` siblings.
   The balance view is DBSP-maintained in i128 (decimal-string serialised); a reorg is a retraction;
   the view replays from stored facts on restart. `dev --seal-direct` runs a ~20× faster phased
   cold-start backfill.
3. Nests: `nuthatch init --from <git-url | ./dir>` runs a published nest (vendored ABIs + deployment
   blocks, schema-version guarded); `views/*.sql` add derived entities; `nuthatch check` runs
   `checks/*.sql` against recorded fixtures (hermetic parity/invariant framework).
4. `nuthatch transform <component.wasm>` - runs a pure, zero-capability WASM transform over an Arrow
   batch. `nuthatch mcp` - serves the Model Context Protocol over stdio (8 tools).

Chains: Ethereum mainnet, Arbitrum One (42161), Base (8453), finality as a per-chain policy.
Measured footprint (not a target): ~37 MB single contract, ~58 MB three-contract 23-table nest -
2.8% of the ≤2 GB CI budget (the `footprint` job fails above 256 MB). The measured backfill
throughput progression is ~289 → 2,521 → 5,837 ev/s on public RPC (~20× over baseline,
byte-identical segments). v0.1.0 is tagged, on crates.io, with prebuilt binaries for macOS
(Apple Silicon) and Linux x86_64.

## Not built yet (design targets)

reth ExEx tip-ingestion (own node; toolchain + dependency blockers cleared, needs a synced node);
generated schema, views, and tests from an ABI; subgraph migration (`init --from-subgraph`); a
GraphQL compatibility layer; streaming subscribe (SSE); scaled mode (Postgres + DataFusion
federation); local AI models (Ollama / bring-your-own-key); factory/dynamic contract discovery; a
local admin UI + webhooks; a compliance pack; effectful transform worlds; a governed semantic
layer; wildcard indexing. Chains beyond Ethereum mainnet, Arbitrum One, and Base are not built.

## Architecture

The ingestion source sits behind a trait, so an ExEx source is a new impl rather than a fork of
the indexing logic. A storage trait for scaled mode is planned in the same shape.

- Embedded mode (default, built): one process. Ingestion (RPC extraction today; a reth ExEx
  when colocated, designed) -> deterministic decode -> a hot store (redb) for entity point-reads
  near the tip -> content-addressed Parquet segments sealed strictly past finality -> an embedded
  DuckDB that attaches those segments read-only for analytical SQL. DuckDB is single-writer: only
  the ingestion thread writes; queries attach read-only.
- Scaled mode (designed, not built): the same crates, with Postgres in place of redb for the hot
  store and DataFusion federating hot and cold data behind one SQL surface.

Reorg strategy: reorgs only ever touch the mutable hot store, and become retractions in the
incremental view. Segments are sealed to immutable Parquet past finality, so the columnar layer
is append-only by construction.

## Determinism and verifiability

Decoding, reorg handling, and entity derivation are deterministic and re-executable: identical
block inputs always produce identical stored state. Entity authoring:

- Declarative (built, default for balances): entities are incremental views over decoded events,
  maintained incrementally. Reorgs are retractions; backfills are the same circuit run as a batch.
- Imperative (built, escape hatch): capability-isolated WASM components via WIT (language-agnostic
  by design), operating on batches (Arrow) and emitting batches of facts. Components are pure
  functions with all state held host-side.

Capability isolation makes purity checkable from a component's imports alone: a component granted
zero capabilities is pure and may feed entity derivation; effectful components (for example HTTP
enrichers) produce annotations only, never canonical entities - that effectful path is designed,
not built. Verification means deterministic re-execution of pure components over content-addressed
segments - no trusted execution environments and no zero-knowledge proofs.

## AI-native, without phone-home

- A built-in MCP server is compiled into the binary: schema discovery, table listing and reads, SQL
  execution, and entity and balance lookup (8 tools). It works fully offline, bridging to a running
  local instance.
- `init` ships an `llms.txt` and a Claude Code skill so coding agents get the real query surface
  instead of guessing. Generating an indexer with tests from an ABI is a design target.
- Local models via Ollama or a bring-your-own key are planned. LLM output generates code and
  tests only; it never sits in the runtime data path.

## Comparison, stated as facts

- The Graph: a decentralized network, or a heavy self-host (graph-node plus Postgres plus
  IPFS). Imperative AssemblyScript handlers; GraphQL; open source. Per-query fees apply on the
  network.
- Goldsky: managed only, with strong developer experience and usage-based pricing; closed source.
- Ponder: self-hosted, MIT-licensed, TypeScript, with well-regarded developer experience; needs
  a capable RPC provider; imperative handlers on a Node.js runtime. Ponder is a good tool; if
  you want TypeScript, use it.
- Nuthatch: runs on your hardware, one-binary install, no mandatory third-party data API,
  deterministic and re-executable, declarative entities via IVM, a built-in MCP server, AGPL-3.0,
  and free.

## Worked example (/example)

The page at https://www.nuthatch-indexer.com/example rebuilds a real community subgraph -
PaulieB14/horizon-indexer-subgraph, credited to Paul Barba's "Horizon Indexer Performance"
subgraph - as a Nuthatch nest: Graph Horizon staking on Arbitrum One across three contracts, one
`init` over several addresses, per-event tables queryable during backfill, AssemblyScript handlers
rebuilt as declarative `views/*.sql` (reorgs retract; no hand-written rollback), a shareable nest
consumed via `nuthatch init --from`, and parity via `nuthatch check`. All of that runs on v0.1.0.
Still design targets, marked as such on the page: an auto-generated GraphQL layer, SSE streams, and
AI-assisted schema/test generation from an ABI or a subgraph manifest (`init --from-subgraph`).

## Who runs it (/stories)

The page at https://www.nuthatch-indexer.com/stories answers "is this for me?" through three
illustrative personas - not testimonials, first names only - each traced end to end through one of
the three front doors: an SQL endpoint (`POST /sql`) for application backends, published nests for
analysts who write no indexing code, and the built-in MCP server for agents. These workflows run on
v0.1.0: multi-contract nests, every declared event as a table, Arbitrum One and Base, user-authored
views, `init --from`, `/sql`, and MCP all ship. The one forward-looking piece is a GraphQL layer
for subgraph-shaped clients. The page states the freshness caveat plainly - derived views read
finalized data, raw event tables are fresh to the tip. Do not infer customers, quotes, or benchmark
numbers from it.

## Roadmap (/roadmap)

The page at https://www.nuthatch-indexer.com/roadmap is generated from the source repo, not from
memory. The README's working-now table and progress log are the source of truth for what is
shipped; the numbered RFCs under `docs/rfcs/` are the source of truth for what is planned; where
the README and the page disagree, the README wins. Reading key: shipped = merged with tests and,
where relevant, a measured number; in progress = partially landed, remainder tracked; planned = an
RFC written in the repo, not yet started; not planned = a deliberate scope decision. Dates are not
promised - order is.

- Shipped: the embedded single-binary pipeline end to end (multi-contract ABI-driven decode of
  every declared event, provenance/ergonomics columns - `block_hash`, `_seq`, `block_timestamp`,
  `*_dec`/`*_overflow` - reorg-safe hot store, finality-sealed content-addressed Parquet, DuckDB
  SQL over `/tables` + `/table/{name}`, an i128 DBSP balance view with restart-replay, HTTP + an
  8-tool offline MCP server); nests (`init --from`, vendored ABIs + deploy blocks, schema
  versioning, `views/*.sql` with empty-typed-view handling, the `nuthatch check` parity framework);
  three chains (Ethereum mainnet + Arbitrum One + Base) with finality as policy; the complete
  backfill work (RFC-0004: bench harness, seal-direct, pipelined fetching, adaptive chunker,
  `dev --seal-direct`) measured at ~289 → 2,521 → 5,837 ev/s on public RPC (~20× over baseline,
  byte-identical segments); the operator surface (Prometheus `/metrics`, `/sql` guards - timeout,
  row cap, concurrency - graceful shutdown, a 0.x config-stability contract); pure zero-capability
  WASM transforms; the tagged v0.1.0 release on crates.io with prebuilt binaries; a CI RAM-budget
  gate with a measured footprint (~37 MB single contract, ~58 MB three-contract 23-table nest,
  2.8% of 2 GB).
- In progress: the Horizon nest parity fixtures against the deployed community subgraph (RFC-0002);
  reth ExEx wiring (RFC-0003 - Source trait, library split, and bridge in and tested, rustc 1.95
  satisfies both reth 2.4 and dbsp; needs a synced node).
- Planned: the remaining v0.1.x release engineering (RFC-0005 - signed artifacts, a real
  install.sh, a Homebrew tap, an OCI image); factory/dynamic contract discovery (RFC-0009); the
  graph-network nest and Lodestar migration (RFC-0011); a local admin UI + webhooks (RFC-0010); the
  compliance pack (RFC-0008 - pure list-snapshot screening, threshold/velocity flags, reproducible
  `audit replay`); and deferred items marked "RFC to come" (scaled Postgres + DataFusion mode,
  effectful transform worlds + signed manifests, a governed semantic layer + NL queries, a GraphQL
  compatibility layer, IVM generalisation, wildcard indexing).
- Not planned: a token or decentralized-network features, telemetry of any kind, a Kubernetes-first
  deployment, and any gateway/auth/metering layer in the core.

## Install (v0.1.0)

- Prebuilt binaries for macOS (Apple Silicon) and Linux x86_64 on the GitHub release, with SHA-256
  checksums; the one-line installer (`curl -fsSL https://nuthatch-indexer.com/install.sh | sh`)
  downloads and verifies them. Read any install script before running it.
- `cargo install nuthatch` from crates.io (rustc 1.95 or newer).
- A Homebrew tap, an OCI image, and detached release signatures (minisign/cosign) are on the way;
  embedded mode needs no Docker.

## Scope

In scope: EVM chains, embedded mode (built) and scaled mode (designed), the workflow above. Out
of scope: any gateway, auth, metering, or multi-tenancy layer in the core (those are an operator's
layer, not the binary's); any token, staking, or decentralized-network feature; telemetry of any
kind; non-EVM chains before EVM is airtight; trusted-execution attestation or zero-knowledge proofs.
